items.prompt_md: What does the expression `i & (i - 1)` compute?
options: It clears the lowest set bit of `i`
It sets the lowest cleared bit of `i`
It reverses the bit order of `i`
It counts the number of set bits in `i`
items.answer: It clears the lowest set bit of `i`
items.explanation_md: `i - 1` flips every bit from the lowest set bit downward; ANDing with `i` keeps everything above that bit unchanged and zeroes out the lowest set bit itself.

items.prompt_md: Why does `dp[i] = dp[i & (i - 1)] + 1` correctly compute the popcount table for every `i` from 0 to n?
options: `i & (i - 1)` is always exactly one bit 'simpler' than `i` (one fewer set bit), so its popcount plus one gives `i`'s popcount
`i & (i - 1)` is always half of `i`, so the recurrence halves the problem each time
It works because `i & (i - 1)` is always zero for even `i`
It works only for powers of two, and needs a special case otherwise
items.answer: `i & (i - 1)` is always exactly one bit 'simpler' than `i` (one fewer set bit), so its popcount plus one gives `i`'s popcount
items.explanation_md: Clearing the lowest set bit always reduces the set-bit count by exactly one, so `dp[i & (i-1)]` is always a previously-computed value that's one bit-count away from `dp[i]`.

items.prompt_md: Which bitwise expression checks whether the `i`-th bit (0-indexed from the least significant bit) of `n` is set?
options: `n & i`
`n ^ i`
items.explanation_md: Shifting `n` right by `i` moves the target bit into the least-significant position, and ANDing with 1 isolates just that one bit's value (0 or 1).

items.prompt_md: Which bitwise expression sets the `i`-th bit of `n` to 1 without disturbing any other bit?
items.explanation_md: OR-ing with a mask that has only bit `i` set forces that bit to 1 while leaving every other bit exactly as it was, since OR with 0 is a no-op.

items.prompt_md: Which bitwise expression clears the `i`-th bit of `n` (forces it to 0) without disturbing any other bit?
options: `n ^ i`
items.explanation_md: `~(1 << i)` is a mask of all 1s except a 0 at position `i`; ANDing with it forces that one bit to 0 while every other bit passes through unchanged (AND with 1 is a no-op).

items.prompt_md: How can you check whether a positive integer `n` is a power of two using a single bitwise expression?
items.explanation_md: A power of two has exactly one set bit, so clearing its lowest (only) set bit with `n & (n - 1)` leaves zero; the positivity check excludes the degenerate `n = 0` case, which would also satisfy the AND but isn't a power of two.

items.prompt_md: How does representing 'subsets of a set of size k' as bitmasks 0..2^k-1 work?
options: Each bitmask is an integer whose `i`-th bit indicates whether element `i` is included in that subset
Each bitmask stores the sorted list of included element indices as digits
Each bitmask represents the size of the subset, not its contents
Bitmasks can only represent subsets of size exactly k/2
items.answer: Each bitmask is an integer whose `i`-th bit indicates whether element `i` is included in that subset
items.explanation_md: Because each of the k elements independently has an 'in' or 'out' state, the 2^k integers from 0 to 2^k-1 exactly enumerate every subset, one bit per element.

items.prompt_md: Why is `i & (i - 1)` described as a 'cheap mask, not a loop' compared to counting bits by repeated shifting?
options: Because it's a single AND operation on fixed-width integers — constant-time hardware work — regardless of how many bits are in `i`
Because it internally still loops but the loop is hidden inside the AND operator
Because it only works for numbers smaller than 32
Because AND is always faster than OR at the hardware level
items.answer: Because it's a single AND operation on fixed-width integers — constant-time hardware work — regardless of how many bits are in `i`
items.explanation_md: A fixed-width AND is one instruction no matter how many bits are set, whereas a naive per-bit scan does one iteration per bit — the mask replaces an O(log i) loop with O(1) work plus a table lookup.

items.prompt_md: A common bug when building a bit mask is writing `1 << 31` (or higher) in a 32-bit signed integer context. What goes wrong?
options: The shift sets the sign bit, so the resulting value is interpreted as negative in signed arithmetic, which can silently break comparisons like `n & mask > 0`
The shift operator throws an error at bit position 31
`1 << 31` always evaluates to zero on every platform
Signed integers cannot store any bit patterns above bit 30
items.answer: The shift sets the sign bit, so the resulting value is interpreted as negative in signed arithmetic, which can silently break comparisons like `n & mask > 0`
items.explanation_md: In fixed-width signed integer types, the highest bit doubles as the sign bit, so a mask that sets it produces a negative number in that representation — a frequent source of off-by-sign bugs in bit-manipulation code.

items.prompt_md: Why does the lesson describe `dp[i] = dp[smaller(i)] + constant` as a 'general recipe' rather than a one-off trick for popcount?
options: Because any 'compute this for every value in a range' bit problem can potentially reuse a smaller, already-solved index found via a bitwise mask
Because it only ever applies to counting set bits, nothing else
Because it requires recomputing every value from scratch each time
Because it works only when the range starts above zero
items.answer: Because any 'compute this for every value in a range' bit problem can potentially reuse a smaller, already-solved index found via a bitwise mask
items.explanation_md: The lesson frames the mask-to-smaller-index idea as transferable: `i & (i-1)` and `i >> 1` are just two examples of shrinking `i` to a previously solved subproblem, the same shape as any DP recurrence.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of building the full popcount table for 0..n using `dp[i] = dp[i & (i - 1)] + 1`?
options: O(n) time, O(n) space
O(n log n) time, O(1) space
O(n^2) time, O(n) space
O(log n) time, O(log n) space
items.answer: O(n) time, O(n) space
items.explanation_md: Each entry does O(1) work (one AND and one table lookup), and there are n+1 entries to fill and store — O(n) time and O(n) space for the table.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the naive approach that counts bits for each number in 0..n independently, by shifting and masking with `& 1` until the value is zero?
options: O(n log n) total
O(n) total
O(n^2) total
O(log n) total
items.answer: O(n log n) total
items.explanation_md: Each number `i` takes O(log i) time to count bit-by-bit, and summed across all n numbers that totals O(n log n) — strictly worse than the O(n) DP-with-mask approach.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of checking whether a single integer `n` is a power of two using `n & (n - 1) == 0`?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: It's a single subtraction, a single AND, and a single comparison — constant-time regardless of how large `n` is (for fixed-width integers).

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of enumerating all 2^k subsets of a k-element set using bitmasks 0 to 2^k - 1?
options: O(2^k) to enumerate the masks themselves, times whatever work is done per mask
O(k) total regardless of what's done per mask
O(k^2) total regardless of what's done per mask
O(log k) total regardless of what's done per mask
items.answer: O(2^k) to enumerate the masks themselves, times whatever work is done per mask
items.explanation_md: There are exactly 2^k possible subsets, so simply looping over the masks 0..2^k-1 already costs O(2^k); any per-subset work (like reading out included elements) multiplies that base cost.

items.prompt_md: You need to compute, for every integer from 0 to n, how many 1-bits it has, and you want to avoid recomputing each one from scratch. Which approach fits?
options: For each number independently, shift and mask with `&1` in a loop until it's zero
Build a DP table where `dp[i] = dp[i & (i - 1)] + 1`, reusing a smaller already-computed answer
Convert each number to a string and count the '1' characters
Use a hash map to memoize the bit count of every number seen so far, recomputed from scratch on cache miss
items.answer: Build a DP table where `dp[i] = dp[i & (i - 1)] + 1`, reusing a smaller already-computed answer
items.explanation_md: Repeated, related work across a range is the tell — `i & (i - 1)` shrinks `i` to a smaller index whose popcount you've already stored, turning an O(n log n) scan into O(n).

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether the 5th bit of a flags integer is currently on or off, in O(1) time. Which approach fits?
options: Convert the integer to a binary string and index into the string
Shift the integer right by 5 and AND with 1 to isolate that bit
XOR everything together to cancel out all bits except the target
Sort the bits and check the 5th position in sorted order
items.answer: Shift the integer right by 5 and AND with 1 to isolate that bit
items.explanation_md: A shift-then-mask is the direct O(1) way to read a specific bit position; string conversion adds needless overhead and XOR-folding doesn't isolate a single bit at all.

items.prompt_md: You must turn on (set to 1) a specific permission bit in a flags integer without affecting any other permission bit. Which approach fits?
options: OR the integer with a mask that has only the target bit set
AND the integer with a mask that has only the target bit set
XOR the integer with the complement of every other bit
Add 1 to the integer and hope the carry lands on the right bit
items.answer: OR the integer with a mask that has only the target bit set
items.explanation_md: OR-ing with a single-bit mask forces exactly that bit to 1 and leaves every other bit untouched, since OR-ing any bit with 0 is a no-op.

items.prompt_md: You need to determine quickly whether a given positive integer is an exact power of two, without looping over its bits. Which approach fits?
options: Check that `n & (n - 1) == 0` — a power of two has exactly one set bit, which this mask clears to zero
Take the log base 2 and check if the result is an integer using floating point division
XOR the number with its reverse-bit representation
Hash the number and look it up in a precomputed table of powers of two
items.answer: Check that `n & (n - 1) == 0` — a power of two has exactly one set bit, which this mask clears to zero
items.explanation_md: Brian Kernighan's mask clears the lowest set bit; if a number has only one set bit to begin with, clearing it yields zero, which is a direct O(1) power-of-two test without floating point rounding risk.

items.prompt_md: Given a set of k distinct items, you must enumerate every possible subset (there are 2^k of them) to test each one against some condition. Which approach fits?
options: Recursively generate permutations of the k items and de-duplicate afterward
Loop an integer mask from 0 to 2^k - 1, where bit i of the mask marks whether item i is included
Sort the items and take every prefix as a subset
XOR pairs of items together to generate new subsets
items.answer: Loop an integer mask from 0 to 2^k - 1, where bit i of the mask marks whether item i is included
items.explanation_md: Each of the 2^k integers naturally encodes one unique in/out combination across k independent bits, giving a direct, allocation-free enumeration of every subset.

items.prompt_md: You need to repeatedly strip the lowest set bit off a number until it becomes zero, in order to count how many set bits it has. Which single expression should you apply in a loop?
options: `n = n & (n - 1)`, incrementing a counter each time, until `n` is 0
`n = n ^ (n - 1)`, incrementing a counter each time
`n = n | (n - 1)`, incrementing a counter each time
`n = n >> 1` only, without any masking
items.answer: `n = n & (n - 1)`, incrementing a counter each time, until `n` is 0
items.explanation_md: Brian Kernighan's trick removes exactly one set bit per iteration, so the number of iterations until `n` hits zero equals its popcount — faster in practice than scanning every bit position when set bits are sparse.

items.prompt_md: You must clear a specific bit (force it to 0) in a flags integer while leaving every other bit exactly as it was. Which approach fits?
options: AND the integer with the bitwise complement of a single-bit mask at that position
OR the integer with a single-bit mask at that position
Subtract 1 from the integer
XOR the integer with itself
items.answer: AND the integer with the bitwise complement of a single-bit mask at that position
items.explanation_md: ANDing with an all-1s mask that has a single 0 at the target position forces that bit to 0 (since anything AND 0 is 0) while every other bit passes through unchanged (anything AND 1 is unchanged).

items.prompt_md: You need the exact frequency (count) of each distinct word in a large list of strings, not just a bit pattern. Which approach fits?
options: A hash map keyed by word, incrementing a counter per occurrence
A bitmask where bit i tracks whether word i has been seen
Brian Kernighan's `n & (n - 1)` trick applied to word lengths
XOR every word's hash together
items.answer: A hash map keyed by word, incrementing a counter per occurrence
items.explanation_md: Bitmasks and bit tricks operate on fixed sets of binary flags or small integer ranges; arbitrary strings with real occurrence counts need an actual counting structure like a hash map, not a bit mask.

items.prompt_md: You want to know if two flag integers share any bit in common (i.e., whether their intersection is non-empty). Which approach fits?
options: Compute `a & b` and check whether the result is non-zero
Compute `a ^ b` and check whether the result is zero
Compute `a | b` and check whether it equals `a`
Sort each integer's bit positions and compare the sorted lists for a common element
items.answer: Compute `a & b` and check whether the result is non-zero
items.explanation_md: AND keeps only the bits set in both operands, so a non-zero result directly means at least one bit position is set in both flag sets — no sorting or extra structure required.

items.prompt_md: You need to find the single value in an array that appears once while every other value appears exactly twice, in O(1) extra space. Which approach fits best?
options: Build a `dp[i] = dp[i & (i - 1)] + 1` table over the array's index range
XOR every element together — duplicate pairs cancel to zero, leaving the answer
Mask each element with `(1 << i)` to track which values have been seen
Use Brian Kernighan's trick to strip bits from each value until it's zero
items.answer: XOR every element together — duplicate pairs cancel to zero, leaving the answer
items.explanation_md: This is a pairing/cancellation problem, not a per-value bit-count problem — the popcount DP and Brian Kernighan's trick answer 'how many bits' questions, while a plain XOR fold answers 'what's left after everything cancels.'
